risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block (the administrative unit above the village), one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B." Comment: This describes an explicit random method (Stata random number generator) with stratification, which is sufficient to indicate a truly random allocation sequence for cluster assignment.

_evidence.method_quote: Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block (the administrative unit above the village), one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B.
_evidence.method_summary: Villages were assigned with a Stata random number generator after stratification by geographical block.
context_clues: randomly selected with a random number generator

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article states that villages were selected using a computer-generated random variable / random number generator and that one author randomly selected villages after stratification, but it gives no description of how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the recruiter. Comment: Computer-generated randomization describes sequence generation, not allocation concealment, and there is no explicit concealment mechanism such as central allocation or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes.

_evidence.note: The article describes random selection/allocation using a computer/random number generator and stratification, but gives no description of concealment of upcoming assignment from the recruiter.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “Naturally, given the context and the intervention, the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind.” “The study was not blind from the point of view of the villagers...” Comment: The paper explicitly states that participants (villagers/parents) were not blinded to allocation, and the outcomes include immunisation uptake measures (e.g. number of immunisations, ≥1 immunisation, completely immunised) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of assignment and associated motivation to attend camps. Although interviewers were blinded, that pertains to outcome assessment, not this performance-bias domain.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Naturally, given the context and the intervention, the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind.” “The study was not blind from the point of view of the villagers, who might have been motivated to attend the camp to ensure that Seva Mandir would not cancel the programme.” “Both surveys were blind: interviewers did not know which villages belonged to which intervention (or control) group.”
_evidence.blinding_method: none; “the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind”
outcome_categories: receipt of all or part of the extended package of immunisation
subjective_outcomes: receipt of all or part of the extended package of immunisation

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Both surveys were blind: interviewers did not know which villages belonged to which intervention (or control) group." Comment: The outcome assessors for the household survey were blinded, and the main outcomes included mother-reported immunisation status plus BCG scar examination; the only non-self-report outcome that was assessor-measured was also assessed by blinded interviewers. The administrative/logbook outcomes are objective records, so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to bias measurement. There is no evidence here of an unblinded subjective outcome assessment.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Both surveys were blind: interviewers did not know which villages belonged to which intervention (or control) group.

outcomes.label: receipt of all or part of the extended package of immunisation
probability of receiving the complete extended package of immunisation
number of immunisations received by children who received at least one immunisation in the camp
probability of receiving all the vaccines in the extended package of immunisation

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The final sample in the household surveys included 2188 children aged 1-3 years at the end point from 2898 households..." and "We used intention to treat analysis—that is, all households were analysed with the assumption that they remained in the treatment group to which they were initially assigned." Comment: The paper reports analysed end-point numbers, but it does not provide the randomized numbers per arm, the amount lost to follow-up for the analysed outcome, or reasons for loss/exclusion, so post-randomization attrition cannot be quantified or judged as biased. ITT is stated, but without observed attrition details this remains unclear rather than low or high risk.

_evidence.notes: Final analysed cohort numbers are reported for the primary end point (aged 1-3 years), but randomised numbers and attrition/loss reasons are not described; no per-arm loss can be computed from the text provided.
